metadata.title: Cancer as Microevolution: Decades of Selection
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05-B08 slates · B09 outro

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Your body has roughly 10 to the 14 cells, dividing billions of times over a lifetime. Each division is a chance for a mutation. By the math alone, cancer should be inevitable — yet it is not. Why not?
beats.visual_intent: title card: '10^14 cells x billions of divisions = cancer should be inevitable — but it takes several independent rare mutations in the same lineage, taking decades'; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: Can Claude trace the multi-hit model and explain why it takes decades?

props.command: claude "Explain the colorectal adenoma-carcinoma sequence: list the mutations in order (APC, KRAS, SMAD4, TP53), approximate years between each, and explain Knudson's two-hit hypothesis and Li-Fraumeni syndrome as evidence for the multi-hit model."

beats.narration_text: Read the sequence: APC mutation starts a polyp around year zero. KRAS converts it to adenoma by year seven. SMAD4 and TP53 loss push it to advanced adenoma by year fourteen. TP53 complete loss by year twenty. Invasion at year twenty-five.

props.code:
# cancer_evolution.py — Claude Code output
# Colorectal adenoma-carcinoma sequence (Vogelstein)

sequence = [
    {'year': 0,  'gene': 'APC',        'stage': 'Normal -> polyp'},
    {'year': 7,  'gene': 'KRAS',       'stage': 'Adenoma'},
    {'year': 14, 'gene': 'SMAD4/p53',  'stage': 'Adv. adenoma'},
    {'year': 20, 'gene': 'TP53 loss',  'stage': 'Carcinoma in situ'},
    {'year': 25, 'gene': 'INVASION',   'stage': 'Detection'},
]
# Knudson 1971: both TSG alleles must be lost
# Li-Fraumeni: 1 hit at birth -> 10-15 yr earlier

beats.narration_text: The timeline unfolds event by event. APC at year zero. KRAS at year seven. SMAD4 and p53 at year fourteen. TP53 loss at year twenty. Invasion at year twenty-five. Twenty-five years of microevolution before a clinician sees anything.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: horizontal timeline with 5 mutation events alternating above/below, colorectal adenoma-carcinoma sequence

beats.narration_text: Cancer requires not one but several independent mutations in the same cell lineage. Vogelstein's colorectal sequence: APC mutation starts a polyp. KRAS converts it to adenoma. SMAD4 and TP53 loss complete the transformation.

props.command: claude "How does APC loss drive Wnt signaling in colorectal cancer? And why does KRAS mutation give a selective growth advantage to APC-mutant cells specifically?"

beats.narration_text: Knudson's 1971 two-hit hypothesis explained why inherited cancer syndromes are early-onset: the first hit is already done at birth. Li-Fraumeni patients with germline p53 mutations develop cancers 10 to 15 years earlier.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'Knudson two-hit: germline hit 1 at birth + somatic hit 2 = cancer 10-15 yr earlier; Li-Fraumeni example'

beats.narration_text: The decades of latency are not bad luck — they are the filtration time. The multi-hit requirement is a protection mechanism, not a flaw.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'Multi-hit requirement = built-in filter against cancer — latency is protection, not failure'

beats.narration_text: The tragedy: the filter is not perfect for every cell lineage in every body. The adenoma-carcinoma sequence is the filter failing, step by step, over 25 years.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 'Adenoma-carcinoma = the filter failing step by step; colonoscopy at yr 10-15 catches it mid-sequence'

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
